title: Asthma, COPD and chronic respiratory disease
summary: Viral respiratory infections can trigger an asthma or COPD flare. Follow the existing action plan, continue prescribed preventer treatment and act early when breathing changes.
evidenceSnapshot: Reliever and preventer medicines prescribed for the underlying disease are more important than unproven cold remedies. Breathlessness, wheeze or reduced response to reliever medicine lowers the threshold for urgent assessment.

interventions.name: Follow the written asthma or COPD action plan
interventions.finding: Structured escalation helps recognise and treat a flare early.
interventions.practical: Use reliever and other action-plan steps exactly as prescribed; check technique and spacer use.
interventions.mechanism: Bronchodilation and control of airway inflammation.
interventions.cautions: An action plan is individual; do not copy another person's doses.

interventions.name: Continue prescribed preventer medicines
interventions.finding: Stopping controller treatment can increase flare risk.
interventions.practical: Continue inhaled corticosteroids and other regular respiratory medicines unless a clinician advises otherwise.
interventions.cautions: Rinse mouth after inhaled corticosteroids where directed.

interventions.name: Influenza vaccination and early antiviral review
interventions.finding: Chronic respiratory disease increases influenza complication risk.
interventions.practical: Have annual vaccination and contact a clinician early for suspected influenza.
interventions.cautions: Vaccination does not treat an existing illness.

interventions.name: Saline, fluids and simple analgesia
interventions.finding: Can relieve upper-airway symptoms without stimulating the heart or drying secretions excessively.
interventions.practical: Use saline products and paracetamol as appropriate; ask before NSAIDs if asthma-sensitive.
interventions.mechanism: Mechanical clearance, hydration and pain relief.
interventions.cautions: Some people with asthma react to aspirin or NSAIDs.

interventions.name: Steam, menthol and essential oils
interventions.finding: May feel soothing but do not treat lower-airway narrowing and can irritate some people.
interventions.practical: Prefer a warm shower or clean humidifier rather than concentrated vapours.
interventions.cautions: Stop if wheeze, cough or chest tightness worsens; avoid burn risks.

practicalSteps: Check inhaler technique and spacer availability before illness worsens.
Measure peak flow only if it is already part of the action plan.
Avoid smoke, vaping, aerosols and strong fragrances.
Seek same-day review if symptoms are moving into the chest or usual control is lost.
avoidOrCare: Do not rely on cough suppressant or steam instead of asthma/COPD treatment.
Use beta-blockers, NSAIDs and decongestants only with appropriate clinical context.
Avoid sedating medicines if breathing is impaired or carbon-dioxide retention is a concern.

myths.claim: Wheezing during a cold is just mucus and will clear with steam.
myths.reality: It may be airway narrowing that needs reliever treatment and medical review.

myths.claim: Preventer inhalers should be stopped during an infection.
myths.reality: They are generally continued; stopping them can worsen control.

redFlags: Severe breathlessness, inability to speak full sentences, blue lips, exhaustion or confusion — call 000
Reliever inhaler not lasting or not helping as the action plan predicts
Marked chest tightness, rapidly falling peak flow or increasing work of breathing
COPD flare with new confusion, drowsiness, chest pain or significant change in sputum
unsettled: The value of home pulse-oximetry varies; readings can be inaccurate and should not override symptoms or an action plan.
